full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I learned something incredibly valuable from one of my chaplain mentors when I was in seminary.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> Dottie was the chaplain assigned to the Cancer Care Center at Porter Hospital here in Denver.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> I learned so much doing rounds with her.<|20.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We would walk into a room, check in with the patient, and make sure that now was an okay<|6.56|> <|6.56|> time.<|7.06|> <|7.96|> And if they wanted prayer, she took their hand, looked them in the eye, and asked,<|15.32|> <|15.94|> what should we talk to God about?<|18.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> She taught me that I should never assume what people want or need to pray about.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> And she showed me that most people are afraid to pray,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> or they think their words are somehow inadequate.<|18.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Those patients' responses to her question,<|3.00|> <|3.00|> they didn't even realize they were prayers themselves,<|6.00|> <|6.00|> and they were some of the most eloquent and heartfelt prayers<|10.00|> <|10.00|> I have ever had the privilege to hear.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> I follow Dottie's example every time I pray with one of you.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> I know better than to assume what you want or need.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Many of you want to pray that your families won't worry.<|6.24|> <|6.24|> Some of you want to pray for the mending of a strained relationship.<|11.24|> <|11.24|> One parishioner asked to pray that her students would do well<|17.76|> <|17.76|> while she was out recovering from an operation.<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I find that people usually want to pray for others, even as they themselves are in a hospital<|9.84|> <|9.84|> bed or about to head into surgery.<|12.64|> <|13.58|> Incredible.<|14.46|> <|15.92|> What should we talk to God about?<|18.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.16|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In Matthew 6, when Jesus taught his disciples to pray, he began by saying, "Your Father<|8.18|> <|8.18|> knows what you need even before you ask him."<|12.76|> <|12.76|> A not uncommon and not unreasonable follow-up question often in catechumenate classes is,<|21.72|> <|21.72|> "If God already knows our needs, what is the point of praying?"<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Jesus didn't stop there.<|3.08|> <|4.00|> He followed that statement with the words of the Lord's Prayer.<|7.68|> <|9.04|> Even though God knows, Jesus wanted his disciples to pray.<|15.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So if God already knows, there must be more to this prayer thing.<|6.44|> <|7.48|> What does it mean to God?<|9.20|> <|10.14|> What does it mean to us?<|11.62|> <|12.74|> And what does it mean to our community to offer up prayer on behalf of others?<|19.74|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> John's Gospel doesn't share this specific Lord's Prayer text found in Matthew and Luke.<|8.12|> <|9.34|> Instead, today we see Jesus modeling a slightly different kind of prayer,<|15.66|> <|15.98|> the kind of prayer we practice almost every week in our liturgy,<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The kind of prayer that is the cornerstone of Daughters of the King.<|5.04|> <|5.76|> The kind of prayer so many of you ask for in hospital.<|10.88|> <|12.44|> Prayer for others.<|13.52|> <|14.90|> Intercessory prayer.<|15.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We pray for a wide variety of reasons, and all of them, all of them, bring us closer to God.<|8.66|> <|9.80|> And in coming closer to God, we inevitably become closer to each other.<|15.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Hebrews proclaims that Jesus lived to intercede for us.<|5.56|> <|5.56|> And Paul assures us in Romans 8 that the Spirit intercedes for us even when we cannot find the words to pray.<|16.28|> <|17.34|> With sighs too deep for words.<|20.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Prayer changes us, and when we are changed, our relationships change, and ultimately,<|11.76|> <|11.76|> our whole community changes.<|15.76|> <|15.76|> Our prayer on behalf of others draws us in together in the communion of saints.<|24.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Intercessory prayer is the acceptance of an invitation into the work that God is doing on behalf of God's people.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> Our prayer for others is a direct acknowledgement that we share in the life of Christ.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As intercessions change us, they truly do change our community.<|8.50|> <|8.50|> I've shared before a quote from Diedrich Bonhoeffer that haunts me.<|14.76|> <|14.76|> It's a caution in his book, Life Together.<|18.76|> <|18.76|> "A community that does not pray for each other cannot, in fact, survive."<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Intercessory prayer creates a space in which we become more compassionate,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> including prayer for the forgiveness of others as we ourselves have been forgiven.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> By opening our hearts to the pain and suffering of others,<|21.00|> <|21.00|> we embody a more profound empathy for others.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we are invited to participate in the reconciling and healing work of Jesus.<|7.94|> <|9.08|> We are not simply saying names on a list.<|13.16|> <|13.88|> We are praying for the smiles, the tears, the pain, and the personalities of those who share life with us.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> not just names on a list.<|3.36|> <|5.10|> In praying as Jesus prays,<|7.26|> <|8.12|> we are aligning ourselves with him<|10.68|> <|10.68|> and his love for all people.<|12.92|> <|14.40|> That is nothing less<|17.14|> <|17.14|> than community-transforming action.<|20.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and community bonding action.<|3.34|> <|3.34|> It is one of the primary ways we have managed to stay so intimately connected<|10.00|> <|10.00|> over this year apart.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> My friends, we do not have our life in an absent God,<|22.00|> <|22.00|> the watchmaker God of the deists who wound up the world and let it go.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we find life in an active, loving, interested, faithful God<|7.20|> <|7.20|> who with Jesus Christ and the Holy Spirit invites us into the holy work of prayer<|14.88|> <|14.88|> for one another and for the world.<|18.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In this text today, on this final Sunday before Pentecost,<|5.68|> <|5.68|> we encounter Jesus praying for his disciples on the evening before his crucifixion.<|11.58|> <|12.66|> He is facing unimaginable pain and suffering.<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yet, his prayer is for others, reminding me of that usual response to the question,<|10.00|> <|11.00|> what should we talk to God about?<|13.02|> <|14.36|> We listen in on Jesus' words as he prays to God on behalf of those who will be left behind.<|22.74|> <|22.74|> We listen in on Jesus' words as he prays to God on behalf of those who will be left behind.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus doesn't pray that everything will be easy and peaceful,<|5.08|> <|5.08|> but that God will be with them, protect them from evil, and give them truth.<|13.06|> <|14.90|> I honestly wish that we were given just a tiny bit more in today's lectionary text,<|21.20|> <|21.46|> because the very next line, verse 20, is truly incredible.<|27.54|> <|27.54|> Amen.<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I ask not only on behalf of these, but also on behalf of those who will believe in me through their word, that they may all be one.<|14.36|> <|16.58|> Jesus here prays for all who are left behind when he leaves and all who are to follow.<|26.56|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, Jesus is praying for us.<|3.52|> <|4.68|> Long ago, on the night before he was to be crucified,<|8.50|> <|9.30|> Jesus prayed for you and for me<|12.14|> <|12.14|> in praying for those believers yet to come.<|16.20|> <|17.90|> And knowing that,<|19.04|> <|20.40|> I ask, as my chaplain mentor Dottie does,<|23.34|> <|24.48|> what should we talk to God about?<|27.36|> <|27.36|> What should we talk to God about?<|28.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm delighted to be saying for the last time,<|6.54|> <|7.72|> be well, my friends.<|9.28|> <|10.28|> I miss you.<|11.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.06|><|endoftext|>
